Independents lean toward Democrats by a 12-point margin, 46%-34%, as do majorities of Black and Latino voters, voters under 50 years old and those with a college degree. Republicans still have an edge among men, white voters and those without a college education.

That 5-point Democratic lead on the generic ballot is essentially even with the NBC News poll conducted in March, when Democrats had a 6-point lead. Looking at similar points in previous midterm election cycles, both parties were tied on the generic ballot in the May 2022 NBC News poll, while Democrats had a 10-point lead in the June 2018 NBC News poll.

“These are rocky numbers for Republicans, but they are not catastrophic,” said Republican pollster Bill McInturff of Public Opinion Strategies, who conducted the survey with Democratic pollster Jeff Horwitt of Hart Research Associates.

McInturff noted that Democrats held a larger advantage in recent “blue wave” years, like 2018. But Democrats also have a much smaller hill to climb to take the House this year: They need to net just three seats, even if redistricting has narrowed the battlefield.
Democrats do face a tougher battle to net the four Senate seats needed for a majority in that chamber, since they would have to win multiple states Trump won by double digits in 2024.
Horwitt, the Democratic pollster, noted there were some signs of cracks in the GOP base in the fight for Congress.

A higher share of voters (95%) who backed then-Vice President Kamala Harris in 2024 say they prefer a Democratic-controlled Congress, while 90% of Trump voters say they prefer a Republican-controlled Congress.

“Does this need to be 2018? No,” Horwitt said, referring to the election where Democrats netted 40 House seats. Horwitt added that Democrats “are still in a really good position, despite redistricting, to win seats.”

Democrats’ advantage in the battle for Congress comes as Trump’s approval rating has dropped to 42% among registered voters, the lowest mark of his second term in NBC News surveys. Trump previously dipped that low among registered voters in July 2020, in the depths of the coronavirus pandemic. (Among all adults, Trump’s approval rating stands at 39%.)
Trump continues to struggle significantly with independent voters, with two-thirds disapproving of his job as president.

Meanwhile, some groups that shifted toward Trump in the 2024 election — most notably young voters and Latino voters — are now registering sharply negative opinions of the president’s job performance. Among Latinos, 64% disapprove and 34% approve of Trump, while 77% of voters between ages 18 and 29 disapprove of Trump, compared to 21% who approve.

Republicans are still broadly sticking with Trump, with 82% approving of his job as president and 58% saying they “strongly” approve. But that GOP support has dropped since March, when 88% of Republicans approved of Trump’s job as president and 63% strongly approved.
The president’s low marks come as voters voice more pessimism about the direction of the country and some of its major institutions.
A majority of registered voters — 56% — say they believe America’s best years are behind it, compared to 40% who believe America’s best years are still ahead.
Those results are similar to when NBC News last asked the question in 2022. It’s the fourth consecutive time a majority of respondents have said they believe the country’s best years are behind it.

In the new poll, about 60% of Democrats and independents say America’s best years are in the past, while Republicans are split on the question. Majorities of white, Black and Latino voters believe America’s best years are behind it. Meanwhile, a handful of groups that lean Republican and more conservative, along with middle class voters of color, believe America’s best years are still yet to come.

There are similar trends across a different, related question, about achieving the “American Dream.” Almost 80% of voters say the American Dream is harder to attain now than it was a generation ago, while 17% say it’s about the same and only 5% say it’s easier to attain now.
The poll also details how voters have also soured on some major political institutions as they prepare to pass judgment in the midterms.

Half of voters say they have very little or no confidence in the federal government, particularly younger voters, while 18% say they have a great deal or quite a bit of confidence. And 58% of voters say they have very little or no confidence in Congress, while 11% say they have a great deal or quite a bit of confidence in Congress.

The NBC News poll surveyed 2,400 registered voters from May 29 to June 7 via a mix of telephone interviews and an online survey sent via text message. It has a margin of error of plus or minus 2 percentage points.

title: Morocco’s draw against Brazil showed its potential to be the World Cup’s ultimate dark horse
summary: Four years after becoming the first Arab team to reach the World Cup semifinals, Morocco arrives with a roster of European stars — including  Real Madrid’s Brahim Diaz and captain Achraf Hakimi, fresh of a Champions League title with Paris-St. Germain.
story: When most people think of the FIFA World Cup, they picture countries like France, Spain, Argentina and Brazil. But Morocco, a nation without the same storied soccer history, is making a compelling case as a dark-horse contender.
Subscribe to read this story ad-free
Get unlimited access to ad-free articles and exclusive content.

Four years after becoming the first Arab team to reach the World Cup semifinals, Morocco arrives with a roster of stars — including Real Madrid’s Brahim Diaz and captain Achraf Hakimi, fresh off a Champions League title with Paris-St. Germain. Their experience is helping shape hungry young talents like Bilal el Khannous and Ayyoub Bouaddi, tenacious midfielders who refuse to surrender possession.
Fans flew in to witness one of the most competitive matches of this early phase of the 2026 World Cup — a 1-1 draw packed with players aspiring to become national heroes this summer.

“It’s a difficult game to start against a great team of good players. We had a lot of intensity at the start, we kept pushing in the second half,” Chemsdine Talbi told NBC News. “Unfortunately, we did not get the second goal but it was a good game to start this World Cup.”
It’s a strong opening for the Atlas Lions, but the expectation is to go even further than their third-place finish in 2022.

“If you’re asking me [if] I want to have the same trajectory — no,” head coach Mohamed Ouahbi told reporters after the match. “I want to go beyond the semifinals. It was a good match. It was one point — we hoped for more, but one is good enough, and we’ll improve.”

The North African national team showed remarkable connectivity on short passes, creative footwork and blistering speed that pinned Brazil in their own field for much of the first half, forcing them onto the back foot from the opening whistle.

The first goal came from Ismeal Saibari in the 21st minute, the product of a stunning transition out of trouble in front of Morocco’s net. After repelling a Brazilian corner kick, Saibari slipped between two defenders to collect a long pass from Diaz and drove the ball past Alisson Becker into the net.

Vinicius Jr., however, had other ideas. In the 32nd minute, he shed Neil El Aynaoui with a flawless strike from outside the box to level the score. Morocco never quite recovered momentum, returning from halftime to face a more confident Brazil that set up camp in front of its goal.

Goalkeeper Yassine Bounou made several spectacular saves in the second half as Morocco’s midfield began to fray, struggling to maintain possession or capitalize on transitions. But the coaching staff isn’t losing sleep over it.

Ouahbi noted that a sluggish second half was to be expected after such a long absence from high-stakes tournament football, crediting his players for their overall consistency while acknowledging that most had been on limited minutes in recent friendlies.

“First match championship, it’s been a while since they’ve had such a high-intensity match,” Ouahbi said. “It’s part and parcel that, the second half, there’s more errors. But after subs you’ve seen some more freshness and we’ve corrected some mistakes.”
Captain Hakimi, who gave Brazil a difficult time in duels along the backline, echoed that measured optimism in his first remarks after leaving the pitch Saturday evening.

“We have to keep the positive things,” Hakimi said. “We’re going to learn from the mistakes that we did for sure, but we have to keep going. We have two games to continue in the tournament and that’s more important.”
Ouahbi assured fans that the team will continue “soldiering on” through the longest FIFA World Cup to date. And those in the stands — and at home — will have every reason to keep watching.

“We showed that we have personality, strong personality, and we’ll continue showing this,” Ouahbi said. “Do not settle for this draw. We’ll deliver stronger results in the future, and God willing, this is what we’ll do. And I can promise you this is not the end.”

title: Coaching the U.S. was a big test for Mauricio Pochettino. Then his World Cup opener went ‘amazing.’
summary: Pochettino, a 54-year-old Argentine, has been on the job for 20 months, but Friday’s game was unquestionably his biggest opportunity to make an impression on a wider fanbase tuning in to watch the U.S. for the first time.
story: INGLEWOOD, Calif. — As motorized carts drove back and forth inside SoFi Stadium late Friday night, their noise interrupting a postgame news conference, U.S. soccer coach Mauricio Pochettino did not try to hide his annoyance.
Subscribe to read this story ad-free
Get unlimited access to ad-free articles and exclusive content.
“Call FIFA,” he told a moderator.
It was, remarkably, about the only thing that did not go his way during an otherwise dream debut coaching the U.S. in its first World Cup on home soil since 1994.

The U.S. had never scored four goals in a World Cup match before, but opened its tournament with a dominant 4-1 win over Paraguay. Folarin Balogun became the first U.S. men’s player since 1930 to score multiple goals in a game. The winning margin of three goals tied for the largest in U.S. men’s history.
“It was a great match, was amazing for our fans to see, to watch this type of game,” Pochettino said. “I am so proud, and we are so proud, because I think we are winning a lot of fans.”

And Pochettino, a 54-year-old Argentine, may also have gained a few more admirers among the U.S. fan base himself. Although he had been on the job for 20 months, Friday’s game was unquestionably his biggest opportunity to make an impression as many tuned in to watch the U.S. for the first time.
That exposure, however, cut both ways. With no other World Cup games taking place at the same time, the entire soccer world would be scrutinizing the play of the U.S.

“I don’t think I’m surprised,” said Christian Pulisic, who set up the first two goals attacking down the left side of the field. “Today, the effort, everything was there. To me, not a huge surprise, but the things were definitely clicking.”

Pochettino isn’t the first non-American to coach the U.S. national team — in fact, he’s the 22nd. But as with every predecessor, his results have been mixed, with a record of 15-10-1 heading into the World Cup. Before this tournament, Pochettino’s biggest test with the U.S. was last year’s Gold Cup. The U.S. made it to the final but lost 2-1 to Mexico.

Coaching in the spotlight is nothing new for Pochettino. He took Tottenham to the 2019 Champions League final, managed worldwide superstars Lionel Messi and Kylian Mbappé while at Paris Saint-Germain, and coached one of England’s largest clubs in Chelsea.

Yet in those jobs, Pochettino could spend long stretches of the club season around his players. Coaching a national team, by contrast, requires maximizing what little time they have during international breaks. Last fall, Pochettino felt the U.S. had begun developing an identity during wins over Paraguay and Uruguay, but also felt the momentum was gone by the spring. He mixed and matched lineup combinations, assessing 67 different players before landing on a final 26-man roster.

When the U.S. team gathered in May to prepare for the World Cup, that began one of its longest, most uninterrupted stretches together under Pochettino — a critical few weeks of preparation that would show whether the U.S. was ready for this tournament.

“That is why we were desperate one year ago to work with the whole team, and we didn’t have the possibility,” he said. “We know very well that if you have the possibility to work three, four weeks with a whole group, that you believe ... these type of things can happen.”

He continued: “I think they need to know us, we need to know them, to understand what we expect from them. When you only have a few days to reunite and to play, you only select players, but you cannot coach. Only in this type of tournament, the World Cup, because you have a preparation two, three, four weeks, I think that is the only moment that we can coach.”

As the U.S. beat Senegal in a late-May friendly and lost to Germany in its final World Cup tuneup last week, Pochettino stressed to players that “the key was to translate all that expectation and pressure” into energy.

They translated it Friday into a one-sided performance against a Paraguay team that had ranked among the stingiest defenses during World Cup qualifying, beating reigning champion Argentina and five-time champion Brazil.
“This is a team that is complex because they have answers to every element you throw at them,” Paraguay coach Gustavo Alfaro said through an interpreter.

A lack of offense has long been a concern for the U.S. men, but from the opening minutes Friday, the U.S. kept the ball on Paraguay’s half of the field, surveying until they found weaknesses. The U.S. took the lead with a Paraguay own goal, a deflection off defender Damián Bobadilla. But the next three were all the U.S., from Balogun’s high-arcing, left-footed shot into the upper left corner to lead, 2-0, to Gio Reyna’s trivela off the outside of his foot in the final minute.

Former U.S. national player Alexi Lalas, who is often critical of the U.S. team, was effusive with his praise, saying on Fox that the 3-0 halftime lead was “the greatest half of group play from a men’s team at a World Cup in history.”

Pochettino cautioned multiple times that Friday was just one of three games in the group stage. But this is also the same coach who has not been shy about his confidence in the team. During a podcast appearance, he recalled a conversation he’d had with President Donald Trump in December.
“He asked me, ‘Do you think, Coach, that we can win?’” Pochettino said. “I say, ‘Of course. Why not?’”

title: Folarin Balogun chose the U.S. over England. It’s already paying off in the World Cup.
summary: Folarin Balogun, born in Brooklyn but raised in London, agreed to play his international career with the Americans in 2023. Three years later he opened the World Cup with two goals.
story: INGLEWOOD, Calif. — Folarin Balogun stood at the podium and flashed a bright smile.
Subscribe to read this story ad-free
Get unlimited access to ad-free articles and exclusive content.
“It’s a dreamy night,” he said to the dozens in the news media looking on.
Teammates and fans would say that, too.
Friday’s performance — two goals in the U.S. team’s 4-1 win against Paraguay in their World Cup opener — solidified why the program spent years working to sway him from signing with England.

Balogun was born in Brooklyn but moved to London when he was only 1 month old. He spent his entire childhood in the U.K. and, as a dual citizen, played on national youth teams for both countries. In 2021, he was called up to the England under-21 squad, leading many to believe he would join the “Three Lions” long term.

But in 2023, Balogun stunned many by agreeing to join the U.S. for the remainder of his international career. It was a monumental result for the Americans, who not only added a desperately needed scoring threat but also beat out one of the world’s soccer powers for his services.
At the time, Balogun cited the incredible love fans showed him leading up to his choice and the thought of being a focal point for the Americans on the biggest stage.
Three years (and only one World Cup game) later, he’s already accomplished just that.

“I’ve always said the fans gave me so much motivation. They showed me so much support,” he said after the game. “For me, the most important thing has always been to repay that. Today is a great opportunity, and I just want to show the fans I made the right decision.”
After an own-goal by Paraguay put the U.S. up 1-0 in the seventh minute on Friday, Balogun extended the lead on a beautiful one-timer in the bottom right of the net from a Christian Pulisic pass.

Moments before halftime, Balogun struck again. He was fed the ball just outside the box, gained control, juked one defender and sidestepped another before drilling a shot past the goalkeeper to push the U.S. lead to 3-0. Fans went wild, chanting “U-S-A, U-S-A” as the whistle blew.

When Balogun was subbed out in the 71st minute, he received a standing ovation from the U.S. fans. It was more than deserved: He had become the first American with multiple goals in a World Cup match since 1930.

“Not much else to say. The kid is insane,” Pulisic said about Balogun’s World Cup debut. “He’s lethal right now in front of the goal. We’re really lucky to have him. Let’s hope it keeps going like this.”
Pulisic added that Balogun provided more than just scoring against Paraguay.
“Everyone will look at the goals,” Pulisic said, but also worth noticing was “the way he’s fighting against these center backs, holding up the balls, getting fouls.”

Balogun wasn’t the only star, however. Pulisic was exceptional before being subbed out at the half, weaving past defenders and creating scoring chances. Gio Reyna had an incredible goal using the outside of his foot in the 79th minute to seal the victory.
New head coach Mauricio Pochettino said it was a total team effort, calling the squad “amazing.” Pulisic went a step further.
“All in all an incredible start,” he said. “But there’s a lot more that we have to do.”

The men will travel to Seattle for their second match, against Australia next Friday, before returning to Los Angeles to face Turkey. The top two teams of the group will advance to the knockout stage.
Depending on how they fare, the U.S. men could be playing for more than a month against the best nations in the world. But after Friday’s victory, they’ve earned some downtime.
Asked what his plans were after a legendary debut, Balogun thought for a second before answering.
“To be honest, I think I’ll probably just watch some Netflix.”
More from the World Cup

title: 12 people presumed dead after skydiving plane crashes in Missouri
summary: All 12 people on board a skydiving plane that crashed Sunday morning shortly after takeoff in Butler, Missouri, are presumed dead, according to Missouri State Highway Patrol.
story: All 12 people on board a skydiving plane that crashed Sunday morning shortly after takeoff in Butler, Missouri, are presumed dead, according to Missouri State Highway Patrol.
Subscribe to read this story ad-free
Get unlimited access to ad-free articles and exclusive content.
The plane, operated by Skydive Kansas City, went down at around 11:30 a.m. near Butler Memorial Airport along Business Interstate 49, according to officials.

The aircraft was unable to gain altitude after taking off and made a sharp left turn before crashing, according to Butler Memorial Airport acting manager Dennis Jacobs. He said the pilot may have been attempting an emergency landing on the highway when the aircraft went down.

Butler County Sheriff Chad Anderson described the crash of the non-commercial plane as a “mass casualty” event during a Sunday afternoon news briefing, though he did not officially confirm the number of fatalities.
The cause of the crash remains under investigation, and authorities believe it was an accident, Anderson said.

A 911 call reporting the incident came in moments before the aircraft crashed, according to Anderson. Local law enforcement arrived to a scene engulfed in flames. Some family members of those on board witnessed the crash, he said.

“There’s there’s nothing we really can say to make it better,” he said. “We just pray for them and their loved ones, and their friends and their family and hope that they can recover to some sense of normalcy.”

Highway patrol officers are on the scene along with the Butler Police Department and Bates County Sheriff’s Office. Both Business Interstate 49 and the airport will be closed for an undisclosed period of time, Anderson said.
Video from the scene shows smoke billowing from the wreckage in a field.

The National Transportation Safety Board said it is collecting information on the crash and “will likely launch a team” to investigate. The Federal Aviation Administration is also expected to take part in the investigation.
Anderson said local law enforcement would begin the process of recovering and identifying the victims once federal investigators complete their initial review of the crash site.
The FAA and Skydive Kansas City did not immediately respond to a request for comment.

title: Sen. Mitch McConnell admitted to the hospital, spokesperson says
summary: Sen. Mitch McConnell, R-Ky., the former Senate majority leader, was hospitalized on Sunday, according to his spokesperson.“Senator McConnell was admitted to the hospital this morning,” spokesperson David Popp said in a statement.
story: Sen. Mitch McConnell, R-Ky., the former Senate majority leader, was hospitalized on Sunday, according to his spokesperson.
Subscribe to read this story ad-free
Get unlimited access to ad-free articles and exclusive content.
“Senator McConnell was admitted to the hospital this morning,” spokesperson David Popp said in a statement. “He is receiving excellent care.”
Popp did not release further information about McConnell’s condition or why he was hospitalized.
McConnell, 84, who announced last year that he would not seek re-election, has suffered several health incidents in recent years.

Earlier this year, he was hospitalized for over a week after experiencing “flu-like symptoms.” Three years ago, McConnell tripped at a Washington dinner event and was later hospitalized with a concussion. And in 2019, the senator fractured his shoulder after falling at home.

The longtime GOP leader has also faced concerns and questions about his mental fitness for office after he several times froze on camera in the middle of speaking and didn’t immediately respond to questions. He has also been spotted this term using a wheelchair to get around the Capitol complex after sustaining a fall there.
The Kentucky senator was first elected to his post in 1984 and was first elected Republican leader in the Senate in 2007. He held the post until early 2025.
Several elected officials in Washington in recent years have faced questions about their mental and physical fitness for office as they’ve aged.
Former President Joe Biden stepped down from his reelection bid in 2024 after concerns about his mental fitness surged following a infamous debate against then-former President Donald Trump.
And on Capitol Hill, the late Sen. Dianne Feinstein, D-Calif., faced questions about her mental and physical abilities for several months before she died in 2023 at the age of 90.

title: Trump, bald eagles and Zac Brown Band: Inside the UFC Freedom 250 Fan Fest
summary: Ahead of the UFC fight card on the White House lawn, thousands descended on Washington D.C. to celebrate the sport, country and President Trump.
story: WASHINGTON, D.C. — Any norm and presumption of etiquette you thought existed within the hallowed White House grounds, consider it shattered this weekend.
Subscribe to read this story ad-free
Get unlimited access to ad-free articles and exclusive content.
Walking around the Ellipse in Washington, D.C., on Saturday, it was as if you entered a festival taking place anywhere around the country.

But look past the Monster Energy-branded main stage and you’ll see in the distance — past the motocross bikes doing jumps and flips-off of a ramp, and past the 600-ton, 92-foot “Claw” — there’s the Truman Balcony of the White House, where tonight UFC fighters will step in before heading down toward the South Lawn Octagon to fight until someone is knocked out.

title: Curaçao celebrates historic first World Cup goal before Germany rolls to 7-1 victory
summary: Kai Havertz scored two goals as Germany pulled away from World Cup first-timer Curaçao late in the opening half and piled on after that in a 7-1 win Sunday in a group match
story: Kai Havertz scored two goals as Germany pulled away from World Cup first-timer Curaçao late in the opening half and piled on after that in a 7-1 win Sunday in a group match.
Subscribe to read this story ad-free
Get unlimited access to ad-free articles and exclusive content.

Livano Comenencia tied it at 1-1 with his left-footed shot through traffic from the center of the box in the 21st minute to give Curaçao its first goal in the tournament and send fans of the tiny Caribbean nation into a frenzy.
It was all Germany after that with the four-time champions regaining the lead on Nico Schlotterbeck’s header off Nathaniel Brown’s corner kick in the 38th minute.
The margin of victory comes well short of the World Cup record of nine goals which has happened three times, with the last one coming when Hungary beat El Salvador 10-1 in 1982.
Havertz connected on a penalty shot into the left goal in first half stoppage time to make it 3-1. Germany was awarded the penalty shot after Felix Nmecha was tripped in the box by Riechedly Bazoer.
Germany wasted no time after halftime to extend the lead with Jamal Musiala scoring on a right-footed shot across the goalkeeper’s area in the 47th minute.
Brown scored in the 68th minute to push the advantage to 5-1 and Deniz Undav added a goal in the 78th.
Havertz’s second goal came in the 88th minute.
It was a strong start for Germany against underdog Curaçao as the team seeks redemption after failing to get out of the group stage in Russia and Qatar following its 2014 title.
Nmecha one timed it with his right foot into the near post just inside the upper box with an assist from Florian Wirtz in the sixth minute to make it 1-0.
The crowd of 68,021 was overwhelming rooting for Germany but there was a spirited contingent of people cheering on Curaçao as it became the smallest country to ever appear in the tournament.
It was the first match of the tournament at Houston Stadium, normally called NRG Stadium where the NFL’s Houston Texans play.
Manuel Neuer, the 40-year-old goalkeeper who came out of international retirement for the tournament, had one save in his first appearance for Germany in almost two years.

The match featured the largest age gap between coaches in World Cup history with Curaçao’s 78-year-old coach Dick Advocaat becoming the oldest ever in the tournament against 38-year-old Julian Nagelsmann, who is the youngest coach in this year’s tournament.
Germany faces Ivory Coast in its next match in Toronto on Saturday and Curaçao meets Ecuador Saturday in Kansas City.

title: TODAY Film Critic Gene Shalit Dies After ‘100 Years of an Amazing Life’
summary: Gene Shalit, the longtime TODAY show film critic with distinctive hair, mustache, glasses and bow ties, has passed away.
story: Gene Shalit, the popular film critic who spent decades with TODAY, has died. He was 100 years old.
In a statement to NBC News on June 12, Shalit’s family said he “passed away peacefully today after 100 years of an amazing life.”

Born March 25, 1926, Shalit was an instantly recognizable personality. Along with his distinctive handlebar mustache, poofy hair, eyeglasses and bow ties, he is best remembered for his love of puns while doing reviews on the show’s “Critic’s Corner” segment.
Add TODAY to Google
Add TODAY to Google

A graduate of the University of Illinois, Shalit became a part-time TODAY personality in 1970 and was elevated to a full-time role in 1973, replacing Joe Garagiola. He remained with the show until he retired in 2010. "It's enough already," he said in a statement announcing his retirement.

In addition to his reviews, Shalit interviewed scores of celebrities during his TODAY tenure. His 1979 sit-down with Carol Channing is best remembered for the actor telling a story about having trouble understanding the British accent that left him in tears from laughing so hard.
"What's the matter with him?" a smiling Channing said as Shalit tried to gather himself.
Add TODAY to Google
Add TODAY to Google
His movie reviews were often punctuated by his use of puns, which became his calling card.

Shalit made regular appearances on classic game shows "What's My Line?" and "To Tell the Truth" and wrote for a wide range of newspapers and magazines, including The New York Times, Cosmopolitan, Glamour and TV Guide. He also wrote and broadcast a daily piece called "Man About Anything" on NBC's radio network for over a decade.
Add TODAY to Google
Add TODAY to Google
In addition, he authored the 1987 anthology "Laughing Matters: A Celebration of American Humor." He also compiled the book "Great Hollywood Wit" in 2002.

"Shalit has performed with the Boston Symphony Orchestra in Boston's Symphony Hall and Tanglewood, played his bassoon on stage in Lincoln Center, and conducted the Pittsburgh Symphony Orchestra in a full concert of classical music. In none of these venues has he ever been invited back," read a cheeky bio of Shalit in the 2007 book "Mazel Tov: Celebrities' Bar and Bat Mitzvah Memories."
Add TODAY to Google
Add TODAY to Google
He also lent his voice as a character named Gene Scallop in a 2007 episode of "SpongeBob SquarePants."
He became pop culture fodder, often being lampooned on “Family Guy” and portrayed by Horatio Sanz on “Saturday Night Live.”
Shalit had six children with late wife Nancy, one of whom, Emily, died in 2012 from ovarian cancer.
Add TODAY to Google
Add TODAY to Google

title: At 82, Mick Jagger Says He’s ‘Ready to Go’ for Another Rolling Stones Tour
summary: After more than six decades, The Rolling Stones still aren’t slowing down
story: After more than six decades, The Rolling Stones still aren’t slowing down.
On July 10, the rock and roll band is set to release their 25th studio album, “Foreign Tongues.”
Rolling Stones frontman and legendary rocker Mick Jagger chatted with Willie Geist for the June 14 edition of Sunday Sitdown about plans for the upcoming album, including a potential tour.
“Well, I’d love to. I really want to. And, I’m ready to go,” Jagger, 82, told Willie when asked if they’ll head back on the road.
“I don’t think we’re gonna do shows this year, but hopefully we’ll do shows next year,” he added.
The Stones last went on tour in the summer of 2024 to perform their most recent album, “Hackney Diamonds,” released in October 2023.
This next album has “a lot of styles in it,” Jagger says. For the Stones, that was intentional: “You hope that every track has got something for everyone. That’s why you’ve got, you know, 14 tracks.”
While four of the tracks were recorded during studio sessions for earlier albums, the 10 tracks made just for “Foreign Tongues” were written and recorded in the span of just four weeks, Jagger said.

Back in earlier years, it used to take months for the rockers to generate an album they were happy with, the rocker said. Now that the bandmates all write more on their own, they can come together and make music more quickly.
But even being over six decades into a career that helped define rock and roll, Jagger told Willie he still feels nervous for this new release.
“You spend a lot of time on (the album), and it’s your baby,” he says. “You want (people) to take notice of what you’ve done.”

title: Katy Perry, Michael Bublé and More Stars Performing at the 2026 World Cup Opening Ceremonies
summary: The FIFA 2026 World Cup will be a star-studded event, bringing soccer players and entertainers like Katy Perry, Belinda, J Balvin, Maná, Alanis Morissette and more together.
story: The FIFA 2026 World Cup kicked off on June 11 and it is already such a star-studded event, bringing soccer players and entertainers together for a global celebration.

In the first game of the worldwide tournament, Mexico won 2-0 over South Africa. Before the match started in Mexico City, fans were treated to an exciting opening ceremony that included Shakira and Burna Boy singing the FIFA World Cup official song, “Dai Dai,” for the first time, as well as a slew of other artists.
But the celebration continues on June 12. There will be two more ceremonies, one in Canada and another in the U.S.
Follow along with our live blog of the opening ceremony on June 12.
This year’s World Cup is being hosted by the three North American countries, each with their own opening ceremony and native singers to celebrate the global event.
Here is everything you need to know, including all the singers who will be performing.
When Are the World Cup Opening Ceremonies?
The first FIFA World Cup opening ceremony took place before the Mexico vs. South Africa match that started at 12 p.m. PT/3 p.m. ET on Thursday, June 11.
The second will be held on Friday, June 12, before the Canada vs. Bosnia and Herzegovina match that begins at 12 p.m. PT/3 p.m. ET.
The U.S. will have its opening ceremony that same day. It will start 90 minutes before their game against Paraguay, which kicks off at 6 p.m. PT/9 p.m. ET.
Who’s Performed at the Opening Ceremony in Mexico?

Shakira and Burna Boy performed “Dai Dai” for the first time during the Mexico opening ceremony. Alejandro Fernández sang the Mexican national anthem, Tyle performed the South African national anthem, while Belinda, Danny Ocean, J Balvin, Lila Downs, Los Ángeles Azules and Maná also rocked the stage.
Andrea Bocelli and EJAE were also on hand to singer the official FIFA World Cup anthem, “DNA.”
Who’s Performing at the Opening Ceremony in Canada?
For Canada’s opening ceremony, Alanis Morissette will sing the national anthem for the host country and Aleksandar Gajić will perform the Bosnia and Herzegovina national anthem.
Other entertainers that will take the stage at the BMO Field in Toronto include Alessia Cara, Elyanna, Jessie Reyez, Michael Bublé, Nora Fatehi, Sanjoy, Vegedream and William Prince.
Who’s Performing at the Opening Ceremony in the US?
Last but not least, the U.S. will hold its opening ceremony in Los Angeles at SoFi Stadium. Katy Perry, Future, Anitta, LISA, Rema and Tyla are all set to perform.
Additionally, Dan + Shay will be on hand to sing the national anthem, while Purahei Soul will perform the Paraguay national anthem.

title: How TODAY’s Stage Manager David Auerbach Landed a Role in Steven Spielberg’s ‘Disclosure Day’
summary: Find out how veteran TODAY show stage manager, David Auerbach, landed a role in Steven Spielberg’s summer blockbuster “Disclosure Day,” and what he plans next.
story: Like Beyoncé, Sting and Rihanna, TODAY’s beloved stage manager, David Auerbach, needs no last name. To everyone in Studio 1A, he’s simply known as “Davey.”

“I am ‘David’ everywhere except for the TODAY show and at the TODAY show, I’m ‘Davey,’ Auerbach tells TODAY.com. “I don’t know when it evolved. I think with Katie Couric, probably, she started calling me ‘Davey’ a long time ago.”

Regardless of how it began, the moniker has endured for most of the 32 years Auerbach has been a member of the TODAY family, all of whom are cheering him on as he steps out from behind the camera to appear in Steven Spielberg’s sci-fi summer blockbuster “Disclosure Day.”

It’s not Auerbach’s first film. In fact, the veteran stage manager has appeared in a variety of on-screen roles through the years including the ‘90s sitcom, “Man in the Family” and “Gravesend,” a 1995 indie movie picked up by Oliver Stone, as well as various commercials and stage productions.

Despite not being his first acting turn, it is Auerbach’s first role after a 30-year hiatus. And it’s quite a return: With Emily Blunt, Josh O’Connor, Colin Firth and Colman Domingo leading an all-star cast, “Disclosure Day” one of the most high-profile movies of the year.

“I got to work with Spielberg and that’s the most important thing to me,” says Auerbach. “It was definitely an experience of a lifetime just to be even in the same room. I mean, he’s Steven Spielberg. He’s the man.”

title: Need a Playlist to Get Pumped Up for the 2026 World Cup? Try These Songs
story: Soccer lovers know there’s no shortage of songs about their favorite sport.

British punk rockers The Fall voiced their gripes about the the game on the 1983 track “Kicker Conspiracy.” Shakira and guest rapper Burma Boy performed this year’s official FIFA World Cup anthem “Dai Dai.” And then there’s stadium singalongs, whether fans are stomping their feet to the White Stripes’ pounding “Seven Nation Army” or singing along on those infectious “woo-hoos” on Blur’s zippy “Song 2.”
Soccer and music have gone hand-in-hand for decades, and this year’s 2026 FIFA World Cup is no exception. Here’s a playlist to get pumped up for the tournament, which officially kicks off June 11.
Follow along  for live coverage of the World Cup opening ceremony in Mexico City.
1. “Dai Dai,” by Shakira featuring Burna Boy

Why not start with the official song of the 2026 FIFA World Cup? Featuring the Queen of the World Cup herself, Shakira, and guest rapper Burna Boy, the title of this track translates to, “Let’s go,” in Italian. Both artists will perform at the event’s first opening ceremony in Mexico City on June 11.
2. “The Cup of Life” by Ricky Martin
Martin’s horn-drenched “The Cup of Life” was a FIFA anthem in 1998, and it still gets the party started today.
3. “Goalkeeper” by Animal Collective
The legendary indie rock band from Baltimore focuses on the burdens of a struggling “goalie” on this 2017 song with its bittersweet lyrics lamenting, “That ball was lost and he stood by.”
4. “Live It Up” by Nicky Jam featuring Will Smith & Era Istrefi
Produced by Diplo, this official 2018 World Cup song features two Americans and a Russian blending rap, pop and reggaeton to get fans dancing in their seats.
5. “Seven Nation Army” by The White Stripes
Stadium crowds go crazy for Jack and Meg White’s foot-stomping  2003 hit with its burn-it-all-down lyrics about fighting off enemies.
6. “God’s Footballer” by Billy Bragg
This poignant 1991 ballad by British folksinger Bragg was inspired by Peter Knowles, a star player for the Wolverhampton Wanderers in the 1960s who quit the sport to become a Jehovah’s Witness.
7. “Touchline” by Rod Stewart
Raise a pint and listen to another downbeat number, Stewart’s touching 1991 tribute to his “Scotsman” father, who shared his “infectious” love of soccer with his kids.
8. “Wavin’ Flag” by K’naan
The Somali-Canadian artist K’naan’s “Wavin’ Flag” became a surprise global hit after Coca-Cola used it in a promotional anthem for the 2010 World Cup.
9. “We Are One (Ole Ola)” by Pitbull featuring Jennifer Lopez & Claudia Leitte
A tantalizing hodgepodge of Afro-Caribbean beats and samba melodies, “We Are One (Ole Ola)” was the official anthem of the 2014 World Cup.
10. “Waka Waka (This Time for Africa)” by Shakira
Shakira delivered this danceable 2010 World Cup song with a video chock full of the world’s best-loved soccer stars.
11. “Kicker Conspiracy” by The Fall

Leave it to Brit punk rockers The Fall get political on this 1983 track that finds singer Mark E. Smith, a champion of the working class, lamenting how the wealthy had come to embrace his favorite sport.
12. “World in Motion” by New Order

Legendarily dour British post-punk band New Order shocked fans when it collaborated with England’s national men’s team to release this cheerful song — which features a goofy rap by British soccer star John Barnes —  for the 1990 World Cup.
13. “Ole Ole Ole (The Name of The Game)” by The Fans
What soccer fan hasn’t sung along to this peppy stadium favorite released way back in 1987?
14. “Song 2” by Blur
Yep, we’re tossing this one on the playlist because those “woo-hoos” are super fun.
15. “Thunderstruck” by AC/DC
The Aussie hard rockers’ pounding 1990 track has been a stadium staple — and one of the USMNT’s  favorite hype songs — for decades.
16. “World Cup Fever” by Air Miami
Washington DC-based indie rock band Air Miami raised a toast to the World Cup in this kooky dance-pop song in 1995.
17. “We Will Rock You” and “We Are the Champions” by Queen
These oft-paired Queen classics from 1977, which feature no shortage of bravado-filled lyrics, get sports fans clapping and pounding their feet.

title: How to Watch the 2026 FIFA World Cup Opening Ceremony in Mexico
summary: The 2026 FIFA World Cup kicks off on Thursday, June 11, with a star-studded opening ceremony. Here is when and what time the show and match start.
story: The day is finally here! Soccer fans around the globe are gearing up for the kick-off of the 2026 FIFA World Cup.

Featuring 48 teams from around the world, the international men’s soccer championship starts Thursday, June 11 and runs until July 19. For the first time ever, the tournament will be hosted by three nations: the United States, Mexico and Canada. And first up, players will face off in Los Angeles, Mexico City and Toronto.
Opening ceremonies will take place in all three locations across June 11 and June 12 with the first opening ceremony held inside Mexico City Stadium.
Mexico City Stadium will become the first venue in history to host matches across three World Cups. The stadium previously hosted World Cup matches in 1970 and 1986.

Shakira is returning to the World Cup stage, along with Colombian singer J Balvin and Mexican pop-rock band Maná, who will all perform during the opening ceremony along with fellow Latin music stars Belinda, Alejandro Fernández, Los Ángeles Azules, Lila Downs and Danny Ocean.
South African rapper Tyla will also take the stage to represent the visiting South African team, while Burna Boy is also set to perform.
The ceremony will also incorporate festive representations of the World Cup Trophy made in papel picado, a traditional Mexican craft featuring intricate designs cut into colorful tissue paper.
Read on to find out how to watch the 2026 World Cup opening ceremony.
When Does the 2026 World Cup Opening Ceremony Start?
The 2026 FIFA World Cup kicks off on June 11 with an opening ceremony at Mexico City Stadium in Mexico City, Mexico.
The opening ceremony starts at 11:30 a.m. CT/10:30 a.m. PT/1:30 p.m. ET, 90 minutes before the Mexican national soccer team faces off against the team from South Africa.
How Can I Watch the 2026 World Cup Opening Ceremony?
Fans in the U.S. can watch the 2026 FIFA World Cup opening ceremony in Mexico City live on FOX and in Spanish on Telemundo.
People can also stream the event live on Peacock, FOX Sports, YouTube TV and Fubo.

title: What to Know About the USMNT’s FIFA World Cup Schedule
summary: See the full USA World Cup schedule for the 2026 FIFA World Cup, including kickoff times, opponents and how to watch every match.
story: The 2026 FIFA World Cup is already one for the record books.
For the first time ever, the international men’s soccer championship will be hosted by three countries: the United States, Mexico and Canada.
It’s the first time the U.S. has hosted the event since 1994.
This year’s World Cup, which takes place from June 11 to July 19, features 48 teams from around the world competing in 104 fixtures.
The U.S. Men’s National Team’s (USMNT) take on their global competitors from June 12 to June 25 in a series of eagerly anticipated matchups in Los Angeles and Seattle.

The 26-member team will be led by team captain Tim Ream along with Christian Pulisic, Tyler Adams and Weston McKennie and other star players. Thirteen of the team’s stars played in the 2022 World Cup. The remaining 13 players are taking part in their first World Cup event.
Mauricio Pochettino, the team’s head coach, told TODAY last month he felt confident about the team roster.
“I think we need to be excited about the 26, and I think we are looking forward to starting up, because I think it’s going to be a fantastic event in our country,” Pochettino said.
“I think it’s a great opportunity to come together, to create good memories and to create a good legacy for the future,” he added.
The USMNT’s World Cup Schedule
The USMNT competes in Group D, alongside teams from Australia, Paraguay and Turkey.

June 12 (6 p.m. PT/9 p.m. ET): USA vs. Paraguay at Los Angeles Stadium (Inglewood, CA).June 19 (12 p.m. PT/3 p.m. ET): USA vs. Australia at Seattle Stadium (Seattle, WA).June 25 (7 p.m. ET): USA vs. Turkey at Los Angeles Stadium (Inglewood, CA)
June 12 (6 p.m. PT/9 p.m. ET): USA vs. Paraguay at Los Angeles Stadium (Inglewood, CA).
June 19 (12 p.m. PT/3 p.m. ET): USA vs. Australia at Seattle Stadium (Seattle, WA).
June 25 (7 p.m. ET): USA vs. Turkey at Los Angeles Stadium (Inglewood, CA)
If the USMNT advances out of Group D, it will go on to compete in one or more of the following knockout rounds:
Round of 32: June 28-July 3Round of 16: July 4-7Quarterfinals: July 9-11Semifinals: July 14-15Bronze final: Saturday, July 18World Cup final: Sunday, July 19
Round of 32: June 28-July 3
Round of 16: July 4-7
Quarterfinals: July 9-11
Semifinals: July 14-15
Bronze final: Saturday, July 18
World Cup final: Sunday, July 19
How To Watch the 2026 World Cup
Soccer fans in the U.S. can tune into the 2026 FIFA World Cup live on FOX and in Spanish on Telemundo.
The tournament can also be streamed live on Peacock, FOX Sports, YouTube TV and Fubo.

title: Mariska Hargitay Changes Shirts Mid-Knicks Game and Twins with Taylor Swift
summary: Mariska Hargitay seemingly changed shirts during Game 4 of the NBA Finals, swapping a black tee for a custom blue “Stevie Knicks” tee to match Taylor Swift.
story: If Taylor Swift and Mariska Hargitay naming their cats in honor of each other’s work — Olivia Benson and Karma, respectively — isn’t enough of a measure of their friendship, Swift seemingly giving Hargitay a matching T-shirt mid-NBA game is quite the gesture.

Swift and fellow singer-songwriters Alana and Este Haim sat next to Hargitay, “Law & Order: Special Victims Unit” star and Knicks superfan, at Game 4 of the NBA Finals. After a 29-point deficit early in the second half, the game finished with a Knicks triumph to mark the greatest comeback in NBA Finals history.

Swift and the Haim sisters sported blue customized Knicks T-shirts, each adorned with wordplay on Manhattan’s home team name in orange print. Swift’s pun of choice was “Stevie Knicks.” Alana Haim’s read, “Knickelback,” and Este Haim’s said, “Knickole” Kidman.
“Girls just wanna have PUN,” the trio captioned a video showing off their outfits.

Hargitay, fresh off her June 10 Broadway performance of “Every Brilliant Thing” at Hudson Theatre, was initially photographed at the game wearing an all-black outfit and a Knicks-colored New York hat.
But she seemingly had a mid-game costume change, courtesy of Swift.
Hargitay was later spotted wearing a matching bright blue “Stevie Knicks” tee, twinning with Swift.
Swift and Hargitay shared many celebrations in their matching shirts, including a big jump for joy while Swift hugged the actor from behind.
Post-game, Hargitay joined the celebrating crowd on the court. Swift also seemed to be having the time of her life dancing with fans in the victory afterglow.

Swift and Hargitay have been friends for over a decade. In 2014, Swift’s hardcore love for “Law and Order: Special Victims Unit” led the singer to name her cat after Hargitay’s character. Hargitay appeared alongside other celebrity pals in Swift’s music video for “Bad Blood” in 2015. Then in November 2023, Hargitay shared photos of the Siamese cat she adopted and unveiled the feline’s name: Karma, a reference to Swift’s 2022 hit of the same name.
In addition to Swift, Hargitay and the Haim sisters, other celebrity attendees for Game 4 included Ben Stiller, Timothée Chalamet, Kylie Jenner and TODAY’s own Craig Melvin.
The Knicks are now one win away from their first NBA championship in 53 years. Game 5 of the NBA Finals is set for June 13 in San Antonio.

title: New Book Celebrating ‘Ferris Bueller’s’ 40th Anniversary Gives Fans ‘Deeper Understanding’ of Classic Movie
summary: The author of a book about “Ferris Bueller’s Day Off” speaks about its lasting legacy in an interview for TODAY.com.
story: Ferris Bueller may not have been interested in hitting the books, but there is one that may have caught his eye.

As “Ferris Bueller’s Day Off” celebrates the 40th anniversary of its release on June 11, a new book by Jason Klamm, “Ferris Bueller … You’re My Hero: The Story of the World’s Most Famous Day,” revisits this ageless comedy and what fans may not know about how it was made.

The movie was directed and written by John Hughes, one of the defining voices of ‘80s teen cinema: “The Breakfast Club,” “Sixteen Candles” “Weird Science” and “Uncle Buck” are among the other adored films in his canon from the decade. While Hughes’ overall influence in film is well-known, Klamm felt like it was time to shine a light on one of his most popular efforts.
“Nobody had written a detailed, in-depth look at a single John Hughes movie,” Klamm tells TODAY.com about what prompted him to write the book, which comes out June 16.
Klamm interviewed 125 people, which helped affirm how much effort went into bringing “Ferris Bueller’s Day Off” to life.
“Talking to that many people is just a reminder of how much deep artistic work goes into making something that seems like a frivolous comedy to everybody,” he says.

Ferris Bueller, of course, is the fast-talking high school student with slick schemes and bon vivant attitude, played by Matthew Broderick, who deftly portrayed one of the more memorable big screen characters of the ‘80s. He skips school and races around Chicago with best friend Cameron (Alan Ruck) and girlfriend Sloane (Mia Sara) while duping his parents and aggravating his school’s dean of students, Mr. Rooney (Jeffrey Jones), and sister (Jennifer Grey).
More about the "Ferris Bueller" anniversary
Matthew Broderick and Alan Ruck on the 40th Anniversary of the Classic Comedy
Matthew Broderick and Alan Ruck on the 40th Anniversary of the Classic Comedy
Klamm is out to prove there is more than meets the eye with this movie.

“I want people to get a couple of things,” he says. “First of all, I think a deeper understanding of how a film is made and how that art form works. And then I think the other thing is to appreciate, first, how John Hughes saw this movie and what he wanted to get out of it.”

Klamm says the film has changed over the years, thanks to the internet’s ability to “spread certain views about the movie,” including the idea that it’s about mental health and, as movie critic Richard Roeper once wrote, that it is “something of a suicide prevention film.”
“That was never John Hughes’s goal. I can tell you that almost definitively. But it has come to mean that,” Klamm says, while adding that “those theories are out there more, and they evolve more.”

When we meet Cameron, he is depressed and Ferris laments how tightly he is wound as he struggles with how to deal with his father, who is never seen onscreen. It’s a battle that comes to a crescendo at the end after his dad’s Ferrari falls out of the garage and Cameron decides he won’t back away from confronting his father. Klamm also suggests the title of the movie is somewhat contradictory.
“Cameron is the main character of the movie,” he says.

“Cameron has the biggest arc, and that arc could be seen simply as a kid who won’t stand up to his dad, to a kid who learns to stand up to his dad, or it could be a kid who, depending on how you look at the beginning of the movie, might be on the verge of some really, really dark stuff,” he adds.
“He might be in the middle of a really deep depression, and by the end of the movie, he’s at the very least, come to grips with the thing that was causing that depression.”
For Klamm, Hughes may not have intended to lace the zany in with some heady subject matter, but it worked.

“That’s why, to me, this movie can be as simple and frivolous and goofy and fun as you want, or it can be incredibly profound,” he says. “And I don’t know that John Hughes intended all of that, but I do think, as a brilliant writer and filmmaker, he was channeling that, whether he meant to or not.”
The movie is a bit of a paradox, too: It is synonymous with the 1980s, but Klamm believes it was made to possess an eternal quality.
“It was designed to be timeless, even though, yes, it’s quintessentially ‘80s,” he says.
“Apparently everybody on set’s goal is like, yes, ‘We know we’re in the 80s,’ but it’s not heavily referential humor, and it is obviously just classic human relationships,” he adds.

The decade represented a shift in film consumption. Cable TV grew and there was an explosion of video stores, allowing people to watch movies on their terms, whether for the first time or a repeat viewing. Klamm says Hughes was ahead of the curve in understanding that “Ferris Bueller” had to endure beyond its box office performance, something he tried to do with all of his movies.

“He said, ‘I need these to live on in VHS,’ which is all we had at the time. ‘So I need these to live on in home video, especially if they fail at the box office.’ So he just was very forward thinking,” he says.

“Ferris Bueller’s Day Off” is a high school movie that plays out primarily outside of the halls of a high school. Klamm says moviegoers love it in large part because Ferris does something everyone would like to do.

“Everybody wants to get the hell out. And once you’re out of school, you want to get out of work for a day. There’s no avoiding it,” he says. “I mean, John Hughes wouldn’t have said this is an anti-capitalist movie, and that’s obviously not what it is. But it is very much like, hey, we all deserve a break. Let’s get out for a day. And you live vicariously through this movie. It’s impossible not to.”
Hughes’ innate ability to relate to the teen experience of the ‘80s is also part of the allure of his movies.

So maybe let’s do this: “I think the thing he did to really make this speak to teens and speak to anybody who has once been a teenager, or even people who might become teenagers soon, as I was when I first saw it,” Klamm says, “was to show ... the different ways we can experience life. As teenagers and we take things very seriously. They are often taken more seriously than they need to be.”

“But he wrote that without judging it. He was just like, ‘Yeah, this is what you did.’ But that wasn’t the joke. (It) wasn’t this person’s taking life too seriously. The joke was usually something else about that. So he respected teenagers. He respected just a general human experience, I think.”

title: ‘Ferris Bueller’s Day Off’ Stars Reflect on 40th Anniversary of the Classic Comedy
summary: “Ferris Bueller’s Day Off” stars Matthew Broderick and Alan Ruck speak about the film in honor of its 40th aniversary.
story: Ferris Bueller’s day off has lasted 40 years.

You may want to rewind the odometer of time like Ferris himself tried to do with Cameron’s dad’s car when you realize that, yes, “Ferris Bueller’s Day Off” is — gulp — 40 years old. The film came out June 11, 1986, and here we are, four decades later, with its core audience of Gen Xers themselves having grown up to become parents and even grandparents.

Ferris Bueller, the charismatic, smooth-talking high schooler played by Matthew Broderick, whose day playing hooky with best friend Cameron (Alan Ruck) and girlfriend Sloane (Mia Sara) while duping his parents and aggravating his dean of students, Mr. Rooney (Jeffrey Jones), and sister (Jennifer Grey), is arguably the coolest movie character of the ‘80s.

“Oh, well, he’s very popular, Ed,” the school secretary (Edie McClurg) tells Mr. Rooney. “The sportos, the motorheads, geeks, sluts, bloods, wastoids, dweebies, d---heads — they all adore him. They think he’s a righteous dude.”
You could say he was the coolest teenager in the country. Just don’t tell Broderick that.
“No, I never stopped and thought ‘I’m the coolest teen in America.’ I don’t think. I hope I didn’t do that,” Broderick tells TODAY.com about playing the character, in a joint interview with Ruck.

“FERRIS BUELLER’S DAY OFF” IS PERHAPS the finest entry in director John Hughes’ catalog of beloved ‘80s teen movies, which also features “The Breakfast Club,” “Sixteen Candles” and “Pretty in Pink.” Yet, there was a moment when it didn’t seem like “Ferris Bueller,” which has stood the test of time — even making it onto TODAY — would ascend to such heights.
“I saw a rough cut. It was Jennifer and Jeffrey and Mia and me. And we saw a rough cut of it, and we were mortified,” Ruck says about his first viewing of the movie.

“I did see a rough cut early at some point at John’s house, I think,” says Broderick, who earned a Globe Globe nomination for the role, a seemingly incredible feat for a summer teen movie. “A very long, serious version. And I was like, ‘Well, this is a dreadful, dreadful movie.’ … It was awful.”

Suffice to say, Hughes cleaned things up before audiences saw the finished product in 1986 and have no doubt seen many times since. The idea of it being serious, though, was not lost in the end. Comedy and drama are often two sides of the same coin and enjoy a symbiotic relationship — laughter can come from pain. Cameron, who wrestles with depression in the beginning of the movie, addresses his poor relationship with his father throughout the film, eventually deciding to confront him at the end after he destroys his dad’s prized Ferrari.

As time has gone on, some people online have suggested the movie is about mental health, prompting fans to reexamine what the film means. Broderick and Ruck, though, don’t necessarily believe their view of it has transformed over time.
“No, I still think of it the same as — I don’t think it has changed, really,” Broderick says.

“Not for me, either, I don’t think,” Ruck says. “The thing that John was really, really good at was he gave these characters dignity. Teen comedies, so often it’s just like they’re sex-crazed doofuses, and he didn’t do that. He was like, ‘No, they’re real people, and they’ve got real fears and real desires.’ And he honored all that, and we knew that when we were making it.”
Hughes’ movies are funny, but they almost always weaved comedy in with drama and earnestness, a characteristic that is vital in the genre and apparent in “Ferris Bueller’s Day Off.”

“Like all good comedies, the underpinnings are serious,” Broderick says. “You could say it’s about mental health or whatever, but he (Cameron) really is depressed at the beginning and has a very tough relationship with his father.”
“There’s, of course, a serious underpinning to the whole thing, but most comedies have that, and we did know that. We thought about all that,” he adds.
In addition to directing the movie, Hughes, who died in 2009, wrote it, which gave him an upper hand in sharing his vision.

“I just think a lot of times when somebody writes it, if they have the ability to be a director as well, directing their own material, there’s nobody that’s going to do it better, usually,” Ruck says.

“FERRIS BUELLER’S DAY OFF,” WITH A wildly distinctive theme (that would also surface a year later in the Michael J. Fox comedy “The Secret of My Success”) by Swiss duo Yello, arrived at a perfect time: mid-June, as schools were wrapping up for summer and kids got ready for the carefree days of summer ahead. It appealed to students sick of school and maybe even adults who wished they could escape work for a day. And its legacy extended longer than Mr. Rooney’s slow, gloomy walk to the back of the bus at the end of the movie:

There’s a longtime ska punk band, Save Ferris, whose name is a reference to the movement undertaken to raise funds by people who really believe Ferris is sick.A quote from the movie (“Bueller? Bueller?”) by a monotone teacher, played by Ben Stein, has become a staple of the lexicon when no one answers a question.Broderick reprised the role in a Super Bowl commercial. Ruck has also lampooned the movie in an ad.Want to buy an Abe Froman T-shirt? Do a Google search and you will find plenty of options.There are stories about whether Ferris could’ve actually done all the things he did in one day.TV shows over the years have honored the movie — heck, the ABC comedy “The Goldbergs” did an entire episode paying tribute to it.In 2025, the National Baseball Hall of Fame acknowledged the 40th anniversary of the actual game Ferris, Cameron and Sloane saw at Wrigley Field.Go into your attic and dig through some old high school yearbooks. You may very well stumble across arguably the most famous line from the movie, uttered by Ferris himself: “Life moves pretty fast. If you don’t stop and look around once in a while, you could miss it.” Former first lady Barbara Bush once invoked it during a commencement address at Wellesley College in 1990.
There’s a longtime ska punk band, Save Ferris, whose name is a reference to the movement undertaken to raise funds by people who really believe Ferris is sick.
A quote from the movie (“Bueller? Bueller?”) by a monotone teacher, played by Ben Stein, has become a staple of the lexicon when no one answers a question.
Broderick reprised the role in a Super Bowl commercial. Ruck has also lampooned the movie in an ad.
Want to buy an Abe Froman T-shirt? Do a Google search and you will find plenty of options.
There are stories about whether Ferris could’ve actually done all the things he did in one day.
TV shows over the years have honored the movie — heck, the ABC comedy “The Goldbergs” did an entire episode paying tribute to it.
In 2025, the National Baseball Hall of Fame acknowledged the 40th anniversary of the actual game Ferris, Cameron and Sloane saw at Wrigley Field.

Go into your attic and dig through some old high school yearbooks. You may very well stumble across arguably the most famous line from the movie, uttered by Ferris himself: “Life moves pretty fast. If you don’t stop and look around once in a while, you could miss it.” Former first lady Barbara Bush once invoked it during a commencement address at Wellesley College in 1990.
That quote is one of the movie’s calling cards.

“I try not to think that I’m as cool as Ferris, or really that I was me doing that,” Broderick says about the quote, which he says at the beginning and end of the film, after he’s successfully outwitted Mr. Rooney.

AS IT TURNS OUT, THE ONLY ADULT who was capable of outfoxing Ferris was Rodney Dangerfield. His comedy “Back to School,” which celebrates its own 40th anniversary this year, opened two days after “Ferris Bueller’s Day Off,” and finished atop the box office that weekend, while “Ferris” took second place. Both films would be among the 10 highest grossing movies of 1986.

Ferris Bueller sits in that rarefied air where everyone (except his sister and Mr. Rooney) seems to like him. A teenager full of bravado was nothing new on TV or in movies, but the brashness of the execution of his plans was. You could make the argument that he set a blueprint for other characters who came later. Zack Morris from “Saved by the Bell” certainly comes to mind — he’s another schemer with a pretty girlfriend, takes advantage of his awkward best friend, is always dueling with his principal and, perhaps most noticeably, he breaks the fourth wall. Ferris may have indeed been an inspiration.
“I’m sure he influenced writers watching that movie,” Broderick says.
“I think there were a number of things that came after that that kind of used that same format,” says Ruck, who also mentions the short-lived TV series “Parker Lewis Can’t Lose.”

Broderick and Ruck had worked together pre-“Ferris Bueller,” starring in a Broadway production of “Biloxi Blues.” They have been seen together in the years since and recently wrapped a new movie called “The Best Is Yet to Come.”

Another “Ferris Bueller” movie, though? Broderick has previously said he and Hughes shot down the idea of doing a sequel, and maybe that was for the best. The day off would be hard to replicate, but it is definitely remembered, appreciated and longed for by moviegoers who identify with Ferris’ quest to have fun.
“The question isn’t ‘What are we going to do?’ The question is, ‘What aren’t we going to do?” Ferris tells Cameron at one point early in the movie.
Forty years later, as multiple generations have grown up watching it, the question isn’t ‘What kind of a lasting impact did it have?’ The question is ‘What kind of lasting impact didn’t it have?’”

title: How ‘Every Year After’ Handles the Romance Genre’s Most Controversial Plot Twist
summary: What did Percy do in Every Summer After? The plot twist became controversial. Carley Fortune explains why she wrote it and why the show had to change the book.
story: With her debut novel “Every Summer After,” author Carley Fortune says she set out “to write a protagonist who was the villain.”

“I was sick of reading romances where the dude screws up and the woman has to forgive him,” she tells TODAY.com. “I was like, I don’t want to do that. I want her to have had made the mistake. And she’s trying to find her way back to the person she was before she made that mistake.”
What exactly that “mistake” is the driving question of the 2022 book, now adapted for the screen as “Every Year After,” which hit Prime Video June 10.

Both the book and show are set in dual timelines. It opens with an adult Percy Fraser (Sadie Soverall), emotionally wizened after about a decade away from her summertime home of Barry’s Bay, Ontario, deciding to return for the first time for a funeral. She reconnects with her teenage best friend turned boyfriend, Sam  Florek (Matt Cornett) as the mystery of why they broke up — and why she left Barry’s Bay — lingers.

That present timeline is broken up with chapters flashing back to every summer of Percy and Sam’s relationship, starting with her arrival at the lake all the way up to that fated mistake made before her freshman year of college.
The reveal of what Percy did comes at the very end of the novel and became so controversial, some readers have written off the book entirely.
Kelsey Mulieri, 21, who runs a book-centric TikTok and Instagram account, says she’s been seeing at least one video about the plot twist every day in the lead up to the show’s release.
“I mean, it’s definitely one of, I would say, the most popular controversies in the book influencer world,” Mulieri says.
Turn back now to avoid spoilers.

title: Ayra Starr Fan Pass & More Info
summary: On Friday, Aug. 7, Ayra Starr is performing LIVE on TODAY as part of the Citi Concert Series! Find out how to register for a Fan Pass and more.
story: On Friday, Aug. 7, Ayra Starr is performing LIVE in New York City as part of the Citi Concert Series on TODAY, and you could be there! Here’s everything you need to know to attend the concert:

Where: Rockefeller Plaza, 35 West 48th Street (between 5th and 6th avenues), New York, New York, 10112
Required to attend: a valid photo ID for each individual in your party

story: On Thursday, Aug. 20, RAYE is performing LIVE in New York City as part of the Citi Concert Series on TODAY, and you could be there! Here’s everything you need to know to attend the concert:
